title: Treatment of Acute Stroke With Dextran 40
abstract:
Treatment of Acute Stroke
With Dextran 40
John Gilroy, MD, FRCP(C); Marion I. Barnhart, PhD; and John S. Meyer, MD
In a random manner, 100 patients with acute cerebral
infarction due to thromboembolism were assigned to two
groups. The patients in one group received continuous
intravenous therapy with dextran 40 for three days; the
others received an equivalent volume of fluid without the
dextran 40. In the treated group, levels of dextran 40
remained greater than 1% for three to five days after the
infusion was stopped. The group treated with dextran 40
showed lower mortality and better quality of survival in
terms of the neurological status. The improvement in the
treated group
was significantly better than
in the
un-
treated group (P<0.05).
Platelet appearance in those
with
acute stroke
showed abnormalities compared
to
normals, and platelet aggregation in venous blood was
increased. Dextran 40 in vitro and in vivo tended to re-
turn platelet features to normal.
Important
considerations in therapy
of
acute
cerebral ischemia are to improve the collateral
circulation to an ischémie area of the brain and to
prevent progressive aggregation of the blood ele¬
ments and formation of platelet thrombi within the
cerebral microcirculation.
One possible method of improving blood flow to
and within an ischémie area of the brain is to re¬
duce the viscosity of the circulating blood. There is
considerable evidence that the intravenous use of
dextran 40 reduces the tendency to aggregation and
thrombosis of the blood elements in the microcircu¬
lation
as well as decreasing the viscosity of the
blood.1'3
The purpose of this communication is to report
results of a controlled clinical trial of the intraven¬
ous use of dextran 40 (Rheomacrodex) in the treat¬
ment of acute cerebral ischemia and infarction due
to thromboembolism. Some evidence of the role of
this drug in inhibiting platelet activity and reduc¬
ing microthrombosis will also be presented.
Materials and Methods
One hundred patients with acute cerebral
is¬
chemia and infarction due to atherosclerotic throm¬
boembolism of the cerebral vessels were admitted
to the study. Each patient had the acute onset of
moderate to
severe neurological deficit without
improvement of 24 to 72 hours' duration when
accepted into the study and hence each patient
would be classified
as
a victim of
a progressive
stroke or a stroke in evolution, rather than a com¬
pleted stroke. In order to exclude patients with
severe hemorrhagic infarcts or cerebral hemorrhage,
patients were not admitted to the study if the
systolic blood pressure exceeded 180 mm Hg or the
cerebrospinal fluid contained more than 300 ery-
throcytes/cu mm. Patients with overt evidence of
heart and renal failure were also excluded. Daily
records were kept of symptomatic complaints, the
neurological status, and fluid intake and output.
The blood pressure was charted at intervals of four
hours.
The results of the clinical examination
were
graded on a score sheet similar to that used in a
previous therapeutic trial.4 The scoring method
allows a maximum of 50 points for a neurologically
intact individual (for example, normal mentation
and speech, 12 points; normal cranial nerve func¬
tion, 12 points; normal reflexes, 4 points; normal
From the departments of neurology, physiology, and pharma-
cology, Wayne State University and the Wayne Center for Cere-
brovascular Research. Dr. Meyer is now with the Department of
Neurology, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston.
Reprint requests to 3825 Brush St, Detroit 48201 (Dr. Gilroy).
Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a University of Pennsylvania User  on 05/14/2015
 motor function,
11 points; normal sensation,
6
points; and absence of seizures, 5 points). Thus, a
patient admitted with a score of 25 due to hemi-
plegia and aphasia, who is reexamined and found
to have a score of 50 points, may be assumed to
have recovered completely.
The scoring system was devised to express the
severity of loss of function following
an acute
stroke, based on the experience of
a number of
neurologists. Thus, changes in reflexts are not of
great importance in measuring loss of function and
the scale for reflex changes varies from 0 to 4
points. Conversely, changes in mentation or cranial
nerve function are more significant in terms of loss
of function and are graded from 0 to 12 points in
each case. The scoring method is relatively simple
and highly reproducible even if the same patient is
scored by different neurologists.4
The method also lends itself to statistical analy¬
ses which were used in this study.
After details of the clinical examination had been
recorded and the admission
score entered
on
a
standard score sheet,
an envelope
was taken in
numerical series from 100 envelopes assorted in a
random manner and the instruction "treat"
or
"control" which
was contained in the envelope
dictated the method of treatment. The randomiza¬
tion procedure produced two groups which were
equivalent in terms of age and total neurological
impairment (Table 1).
The patients in the treated group received
a
rapid intravenous injection of 500 ml of dextran 40
over an interval of one hour (the loading dose).
This infusion was followed by a slow intravenous
infusion of dextran 40 at the rate of 500 ml every
12 hours (the maintenance dose). Treatment with
infusions of dextran 40 was continued for 72 hours
and then stopped.
The patients in the "control" group received a
rapid injection of 500 ml of 5% dextrose in 0.2N
saline injected intravenously over an interval of
one hour. This infusion was followed by
a slow
intravenous infusion of 500 ml of 5% dextrose in
0.2N saline every 12 hours for the same period of
time. Both treated and control patients received
comparable medical treatment including supple¬
mentary intravenously given fluids if this
was
deemed necessary on the basis of daily examina¬
tions and reports of serum electrolyte values. Vaso¬
dilator and anticoagulant drugs
were not given
during the trial period which was ten days. The
same type of nursing care was maintained through¬
out the study for patients in both groups.
At the beginning and end of the ten-day trial
period, the scores for the neurological examinations
were entered onto the score sheet. Cerebral angi-
ography was performed in those patients who sur¬
vived at the end of the ten-day therapeutic trial
unless there was some medical contraindication.
On the basis of clinical and artériographie findings,
the patients were categorized into two groups for
purposes of analysis:
one group with occlusion of

vessels
and

with

oc¬

Laboratory Studies
Standard laboratory tests were performed on all
patients. Tests included complete blood cell count,
urinalysis, fasting
blood glucose determination,
electrocardiogram, and electroencephalogram. Blood
urea nitrogen levels were determined daily since
dextran 40 has been reported to be a rare cause of
oluguria and anuria, presumably due to impairment
of renal tubular function.
Blood concentrations of dextran 40 in 23 patients
of the treated group were determined daily by add¬
ing 1 ml citrated plasma to 5 ml saline. The solu¬
tion was deproteinized by the addition of 0.4 ml
50% trichloroacetic acid, shaken vigorously, and
centrifuged for ten minutes. The supernatant was
then mixed with an equal amount of acetone and
the resulting turbidity was measured with a spec-
trophotometer. The readings for optical density
were converted to values for dextran 40 per milli-
liter plasma by the use of a calibration curve pre¬
pared by adding
1 through 40 mg aliquots of
dextran 40 per milliliter citrated plasma. This test
is rapid, sensitive, and reproducible.
Changes in the morphological characteristics of
platelets were determined by examining prepara¬
tions of platelets collected on thermoplastic resin
(Formuar) films by the use of an electron micro¬
scope. The method for obtaining preparations of
platelets on these films has been described in detail
elsewhere.5 It was found that platelets could be
classified into four types according to their surface
features; round (even surface), abortive dendritic
(one or more tiny pseudopods), dendritic (long and
numerous pseudopods), and spread forms. Differ¬
ential cell counts of at least 200 platelets were
made, and the extent of platelet aggregation was
determined by counting the number of aggregates
per 100 individual platelets and by assessing the
size and constitution of the platelet aggregates. The
differential platelet count of normal individuals,
which was used for comparison with the stroke pa¬
tients averaged 1% round, 96% dendritic, and 3%
spread forms with fewer than 25 aggregates per 100
platelets counted. These aggregates rarely
con¬
tained more than two or three platelets.
^
Results
Clinical Results—As shown in Table 2 there were

For this analysis, the patient's score on admission
was subtracted from his score at the end of ten
days. If this difference was positive, it represented
an improvement. If 0 or negative, it reflected no
or deterioration. Because these different
scores were not normally distributed,
Wallis nonparametric test was used. The results of
these analyses are shown in Table 3. An inspection
of this Table shows that the treated group im¬
proved significantly in regard to mentation, reflexes,
and total neurological status. There were no differ¬
ences in regard to other aspects of neurological

Note many platelet aggregations and spread forms before treatment and in¬
crease in dendritic form after. Larger amorphous mass (right) is red blood
were no instances of oliguria or anuria among pa¬
cerebral ischemia and infarction differ from normal
subjects in their ability to metabolize and excrete
dextran 40. The blood levels remained elevated
longer than those reported for normals. There was
also considerable variation from patient to patient
in the clearance of dextran 40 from the blood. The
majority of treated patients achieved plasma levels
of 1 % within six hours after infusion. Plasma levels
of 1.3% to 1.9% were reached at various times during
the three days of treatment, and levels above or near
1% persisted from three to five days after the in¬
fusion was discontinued. The variation from patient
to patient as well as from normals is presumed to
indicate impaired renal and possibly liver function
in patients with advanced arteriosclerosis.
The decline in circulating dextran 40 content
was measured in 14 patients in this study. There
was a half-life for the drug which varied from 1.5
to 6.6 days with an average of 3 days.
Changes in Platelet Function and Features.—
There is considerable evidence that a hypercoagul-
able state exists in patients with acute cerebral
infarction or ischemia due to thromboembolism.6
The term "hypercoagulable state," however, covers
mechanism. The present study was concerned pri¬
marily with abnormalities in the appearance of
platelets as an indicator of the state of coagulation
of the blood. Results of this analysis will now be
Platelet Morphologic Characteristics in Acute
Stroke Patients Compared to Normals.—Citrated
platelets from venous blood of patients with acute
were compared with those from normal
individuals after surface activation on thermoplastic
resin film. More than 90% of the
platelets in the venous blood of
normal individuals were present in
the dendritic form having several
presented in the spread form. On
admission to the study, platelets
from patients with acute stroke
activation of platelets since there
was a higher percentage of spread
acute stroke showed 50% to 90%
of the platelets were in the spread
form and there were considerably
more aggregates of platelets than
occurred in normal controls (an
excess of 50 aggregates per 100
platelets). There were numerous

that platelets from patients with an acute stroke
show a greater response to surface activation, it is
also likely that the platelets exist in the circulation
in a more activated condition. The immediate fixing
of blood platelets with buffered gluteraldehyde is
believed to provide specimens which closely
semble those in the circulation. A comparison of 13
specimens obtained from patients with acute stroke
prior to treatment with dextran 40 and specimens
obtained from healthy adults showed more den¬
dritic and spread forms in the stroke patients (Fig
1). In the healthy adult 70% of the platelets were
devoid of membrane alterations and were classified
as round forms. This contrasted with the finding of
80% platelets with dendrites and 5% spread forms
in the patients with acute stroke.
In Vitro Restoration of Platelets Toward Normal
by Dextran 40.—Specimens of venous blood were
taken from 13 patients with acute cerebral infarc¬
tion prior to treatment. The specimens were sep¬
arated into two samples for comparison of activation
on enamel film. A mixture of 0.5% to 0.8% dextran
40 was added to one, the other acted as a control.
In every case, the appearance of the platelets in the
specimen treated with dextran returned toward
normal while the control specimen remained un¬
In Vivo Effect of Dextran 40.—There was also
a change towards normal in the appearance of the
platelets in serial specimens obtained from patients
during treatment of cerebral infarction with dex¬
tran 40 (Fig 2). The number of aggregates of plate¬
lets declined during the three days of therapy and
the aggregates themselves contained fewer plate¬
lets. In addition, the number of dendritic forms
increased. The specimens obtained after the infu¬
sion of dextran 40 had been discontinued showed
signs of reactivation of platelets when the plasma
level of dextran 40 fell below 1%. However, plate¬
let activity never attained the high levels seen prior
Dextran 40 and Thrombin Activation of Plate¬
lets.—It has been shown that aggregates of platelets
tend to cause temporary occlusion of the micro-
aggregates are activated further by contact with
factors such as thrombin.5 The response of platelet
suspensions to thrombin is extremely sensitive.
Changes in platelet features are apparent by elec¬
tron microscopy with concentrations
lxlO10 Iowa units/ml. This minute concentration
of thrombin is about one millionth of that necessary
to transform fibrinogen to fibrin.
When dextran 40 was added to preparations of
normal platelets in vitro prior to the addition of
various concentrations of thrombin (0.1 to 0.001/ig/
ml) there was partial or complete protection from
platelet activation by thrombin. The protective in¬
fluence of dextran 40 against thrombin activation
of platelets was readily apparent in concentrations
similar to those measured during therapy of pa¬
tients with acute cerebral ischemia.
The clinical assessment of two comparable series
of patients with acute stroke in which one group
was treated with intravenous infusion of dextran 40
and the other with a comparable volume of intra¬
venously administered fluids but without the dex¬
tran added showed that the survival rate and the
quality of survival was better in the group treated
with dextran 40. Satisfactory blood levels of dex¬
tran 40 were achieved in all patients in whom meas¬
urements were made. Blood levels were measured
daily not only during the three days of therapy
but were also measured at intervals for a total of
Although the rate of clearance of dextran 40 from
the blood varied from patient to patient, the re¬
sults indicate that therapeutic blood levels were
achieved by the rapid intravenous infusion of a
loading dose of 500 ml of dextran 40 for one hour
followed by a slow infusion thereafter of 500 ml
every 12 hours. There were no complications in the
series reported here so that it appears that it would
be reasonably safe to continue dextran 40 infusion
for longer periods (eg, up to seven days), although
the risk of oliguria and renal failure should be borne
in mind. Longer therapy with dextran 40 might
result in greater degrees of clinical improvement
than those reported here. In any case, it is recom¬
mended that levels of dextran 40 in the blood
should be measured daily during periods of
The Nature of the Beneficial Effect of Dextran
40—The decrease in regional cerebral blood flow
and hence the degree of cerebral infarction that
results from occlusion of a cerebral vessel is influ¬
enced by a number of factors including the perfu¬
sion pressure, the aggregation of erythrocytes and
as changes in viscosity of the
blood in the microcirculation of the ischémie zone
due to hemoconcentration and loss of fluid from
the damaged blood vessels.3 Hypoxie damage to the
walls of cerebral blood vessels leads to edema with
compression of capillaries and venules resulting in
impaired gaseous exchange, further tissue hypoxia,
and a vicious cycle causing progressive infarction.
Dextran 40 appears to be capable of reversing some
of these changes, particularly the tendency for ag¬
gregation of blood elements and increased blood
viscosity in the ischémie zone.
Reports have indicated that dextran 40 and other
hemodiluting agents can prevent cerebral infarction
in animals following experimental occlusion of cere¬
bral blood vessels or can minimize the extent of
cerebral infarction following this procedure.7 The
beneficial effect of dextran 40 may be due to the
following causes: (1) reduction of blood viscosity
by increasing the plasma volume and lowering the
hematocrit value, (2) reduction of aggregation of
blood elements, (3) reduction of vascular resistance
in the microcirculation of the
ischemia, and (4) reduction of activation of plate¬
The present investigation has been concerned
primarily with the effect of dextran 40 on platelets
in patients with acute cerebral infarction. Certain
effects of various forms of dextran on platelet func¬
tion have been reported by several authors. Dex-
trans inhibit factor 3 activity in platelet suspensions
with reduction of prothrombin activation.8 As
result, prothrombin consumption and clot retrac¬
tion are delayed in the presence of dextran.9
Dextrans labelled with radioactive carbon ("C)
have been shown to be absorbed on human blood
platelets.10 Microelectrophoretic studies11 and the
correction of platelet factor 3 suppression by Bonifi¬
cation12 support the view that dextran affects the
platelet surface. The electron microscopic appear¬
ance of whole blood platelets exposed to dextran in
vitro and in vivo are consistent with surface coating
by dextran.13 Inhibition of platelet agglutinability,1
decrease in adhesiveness,15 and suppression of the
release of adenosine triphosphate from platelets"'
have all been reported to be caused by dextran 40.
Electron microscopic studies have also demonstrated
that dextran 40 tends to prevent platelet activation
by thrombin and fibrinolytic end products.
The demonstration of increased platelet activa¬
tion in the venous blood of patients with acute
stroke is a further indication of the hypercoagu-
lable state that exists in this condition. The fact
that this abnormality can be improved in vitro by
the addition of dextran 40 to platelet preparations
from patients with cerebral infarction suggests that
the infusion of dextran 40 may produce similar
effects on platelet aggregates and aggregation of
other blood elements in areas of cerebral ischemia.
Although the exact mechanism of the dextran 40
is unknown, considerable evidence exists that it
coats platelets. This coating may prevent activation
by thrombin and may strengthen the platelet mem¬
In this study clinical results indicated that dex¬
tran 40 reduced the extent of cerebral infarction.
These results are compatible with the laboratory
findings and further suggest that this form of treat¬
ment decreases blood viscosity and increases cere¬
bral blood flow to an ischémie area.
present series, the authors have treated one patient
with long-standing and severe hypertension asso¬
ciated with acute cerebral infarction after comple¬
tion of this study. Oliguria developed on the second
prompt diuresis when the infusion was discontinued.
The occurrence of this complication is rare.1718

JAMES BLUNDELL (1790-1877), proponent of physiological
concepts and teacher of the conservative practice of obstetrics, was
born in London. He prepared for practice in Edinburgh and Lon¬
don, received his MD degree from the University of Edinburgh
and in 1818 was admitted a licentiate of the Royal College of Phy¬
sicians. He lectured in the united medical schools of St. Thomas'
and Guy's hospitals. A notable contribution was his investigations
on the transfusion of whole blood by the syringe in a series of dogs.
He stressed the need for rapid execution of the procedure to pre¬
vent clotting, the importance of avoiding the introduction of air
into the vein of the recipient and the incompatibility of heterolo-
gous donors. In his obstetrical practice and teaching he urged
against unnecessary interference, differentiated between placenta
praevia and accidental hemorrhage and was cautious in employing
forceps. He prepared popular texts on obstetrics and diseases of
women, each went into several editions. (Composite by G. Bako)
